section.title: Overview
section.para: The rust_acl module provides a live-reload file-based access control list for blocking or allowing &sip; traffic by source &ip;, User-Agent, domain, or any arbitrary string value. Both a blocklist and an optional allowlist file are loaded at startup into each worker process. Matching can be exact (HashSet lookup), prefix-based (linear scan of prefixes), regex-based (compiled patterns), CIDR range-based (subnet matching), glob-based (wildcard patterns), or auto-detected per entry.

When an allowlist is configured, it takes precedence over the blocklist: a value present in both lists is allowed. Temporary entries can be added at runtime via auto_block() and auto_allow() with TTL-based expiry.

In addition to the generic catch-all files, the module supports typed files for specific check categories: IP addresses, User-Agent strings, and domains. Typed check functions (e.g. check_blocklist_ip()) query only the relevant typed file plus the generic file, while the generic check functions query all files.

section.title: How it works
section.para: At worker initialization, each process loads the blocklist file (and optional allowlist file) and parses them line-by-line. Blank lines and lines starting with # are skipped.

In exact mode, entries are stored in a HashSet for O(1) lookup. In prefix mode, entries are stored as a list of prefixes checked via starts_with(). In regex mode, entries are compiled as regular expression patterns at load time; invalid patterns are skipped with a warning. In cidr mode, entries are parsed as CIDR ranges (e.g. 10.0.0.0/24) and checked via subnet mask comparison. Both IPv4 and IPv6 are supported. In glob mode, entries are glob patterns where * matches any sequence of characters and ? matches exactly one character; matching is case-insensitive.

In auto mode, the entry type is detected per-line at load time. Entries containing / followed by digits with an IP-like prefix are parsed as CIDR; entries delimited by / on both ends are compiled as regex; entries containing * or ? are treated as globs; everything else is an exact string match. This allows a single file to contain a mix of IPs, CIDRs, FQDNs, and glob/regex patterns.

The check_access() function implements the full precedence logic: auto-allow entries are checked first, then all allowlist files (generic + typed), then auto-block entries, then all blocklist files (generic + typed). If nothing matches, the default is to allow.
Both lists can be reloaded at runtime using blocklist_reload() and allowlist_reload() without restarting &osips;. These functions reload the generic file and all configured typed files.

section.title: &osips; Modules
section.para:
The following modules must be loaded before this module:
None.

section.title: External Libraries or Applications
section.para:
The following libraries or applications must be installed before running &osips; with this module loaded:
None.

section.title: blocklist_file (string)
section.para: Path to the generic blocklist file. One entry per line. Lines starting with # are treated as comments. Blank lines are ignored. This parameter is required. Entries in this file are checked by all check functions.

para.emphasis: Default value is None (must be set).

section.title: allowlist_file (string)
section.para: Path to the generic allowlist file (optional). Same format as the blocklist. When configured, entries in the allowlist take precedence over the blocklist in check_access(). Entries in this file are checked by all check functions.

section.title: blocklist_ip_file (string)
section.para: Path to an IP-specific blocklist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_blocklist_ip() and the generic check_blocklist() / check_access().

section.title: blocklist_ua_file (string)
section.para: Path to a User-Agent-specific blocklist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_blocklist_ua() and the generic check_blocklist() / check_access().

section.title: blocklist_domain_file (string)
section.para: Path to a domain-specific blocklist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_blocklist_domain() and the generic check_blocklist() / check_access().

section.title: allowlist_ip_file (string)
section.para: Path to an IP-specific allowlist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_allowlist_ip() and the generic check_allowlist() / check_access().

section.title: allowlist_ua_file (string)
section.para: Path to a User-Agent-specific allowlist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_allowlist_ua() and the generic check_allowlist() / check_access().

section.title: allowlist_domain_file (string)
section.para: Path to a domain-specific allowlist file (optional). Entries in this file are checked only by check_allowlist_domain() and the generic check_allowlist() / check_access().

section.title: match_mode (string)
section.para:
The matching algorithm to use for all lists:
exact - value must match an entry exactly (case-sensitive). O(1) lookups.
prefix - value must start with an entry (case-sensitive). Linear prefix scan.
regex - each entry is compiled as a regular expression pattern at file load time. On check, all compiled patterns are tested against the value using is_match(). Matching is case-sensitive by default; use the inline (?i) flag for case-insensitive matching. Invalid regex patterns are skipped with a warning and do not prevent the rest of the file from loading.
cidr - each entry is parsed as a CIDR range (e.g. 10.0.0.0/24, 2001:db8::/32). On check, the value is parsed as an &ip; address and tested against all ranges via subnet mask comparison. Both IPv4 and IPv6 are supported. Invalid CIDR entries are skipped with a warning.
glob - each entry is a glob pattern. * matches zero or more characters, ? matches exactly one character. Matching is case-insensitive. Useful for domain blocking (*.evil.com) or IP range shorthand (192.168.*).
auto - each entry's type is auto-detected at file load time. Entries are classified as CIDR (contains / + digits with an IP prefix), regex (delimited by /), glob (contains * or ?), or exact string match. This allows a single file to contain a mix of entry types.

section.title: track_counters (integer)
section.para: Enable per-entry match counters. When enabled, the module tracks how many times each blocklist/allowlist entry was matched. The counters can be retrieved via access_entry_stats().
Since counter tracking adds a small overhead per check (hash map lookup and increment), it is disabled by default.

section.title: access_policy (string)
section.para:
Controls the precedence logic used by check_access():
allowlist-first (default) - if in allowlist, allow; else if in blocklist, block; else allow.
blocklist-first - if in blocklist, block; else if in allowlist, allow; else allow.
allowlist-only - only check the allowlist. In list, allow; not in list, block.
blocklist-only - only check the blocklist. In list, block; not in list, allow.
Unknown values are rejected at startup with an error.

section.title: publish_events (integer)
section.para: Enable event publishing. When set to 1, the module publishes significant events as NOTICE-level structured log messages that operators can consume via syslog or log aggregation. If the NATS module is loaded, events are also published to NATS subjects under opensips.events.*.
Events published by this module: E_ACL_BLOCKED (on blocklist match) and E_ACL_ALLOWED (on access check pass). Each event includes a JSON payload with relevant details.
Check whether the given value matches any entry in ALL blocklist files (generic + IP + UA + domain) or auto-block map.
Return codes:
This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE, FAILURE_ROUTE, and ONREPLY_ROUTE.
Check whether the given IP matches an entry in the IP-typed blocklist file (blocklist_ip_file) or the generic blocklist file, or the auto-block map.
Return codes: same as check_blocklist().
Check whether the given User-Agent matches an entry in the UA-typed blocklist file (blocklist_ua_file) or the generic blocklist file, or the auto-block map.
Return codes: same as check_blocklist().
Check whether the given domain matches an entry in the domain-typed blocklist file (blocklist_domain_file) or the generic blocklist file, or the auto-block map.
Return codes: same as check_blocklist().
Check whether the given value matches ALL allowlist files (generic + IP + UA + domain) or the auto-allow map.
Return codes:

listitem.para: -1 - value is NOT in the allowlist.

section.para: Check whether the given IP matches an entry in the IP-typed allowlist file (allowlist_ip_file) or the generic allowlist file, or the auto-allow map.
Return codes: same as check_allowlist().
Check whether the given User-Agent matches an entry in the UA-typed allowlist file (allowlist_ua_file) or the generic allowlist file, or the auto-allow map.
Return codes: same as check_allowlist().
Check whether the given domain matches an entry in the domain-typed allowlist file (allowlist_domain_file) or the generic allowlist file, or the auto-allow map.
Return codes: same as check_allowlist().

Combined access check using the configured access_policy. The default allowlist-first policy checks: auto-allow, then all allowlist files, then auto-block, then all blocklist files. If nothing matches, the default is to allow. See access_policy for other precedence options.
Return codes:
Re-read ALL blocklist files (generic + IP + UA + domain) from disk and atomically swap the in-memory data.
Re-read ALL allowlist files (generic + IP + UA + domain) from disk and atomically swap the in-memory data.
Add a temporary blocklist entry that expires after ttl_secs seconds.

example.title: auto_block usage
example.programlisting: ... # Block source IP for 5 minutes auto_block("$si", "300"); ...

section.para: Add a temporary allowlist entry that expires after ttl_secs seconds.

example.title: auto_allow usage
example.programlisting: ... # Allow trusted IP for 1 hour auto_allow("$si", "3600"); ...

section.para: Retrieve module statistics as a JSON string. Sets $var(acl_stats) with a JSON object containing all tracked counters for the current worker process.

Retrieve per-entry match counters as a JSON array. Sets $var(acl_entry_stats) with a JSON array of the top 100 most-matched entries, sorted by count descending. Each element contains entry (the matched pattern) and count (number of matches).
Requires track_counters to be enabled. If counters are disabled, returns an error JSON string.

Retrieve module statistics in Prometheus text exposition format. Sets $var(acl_prom) with a multi-line string containing all counters formatted with # HELP, # TYPE, and metric value lines. Counters use the Prometheus counter type; gauges like entries_blocklist use the gauge type.
This output can be exposed via httpd or a custom MI command for scraping by a Prometheus server.

example.title: access_prometheus usage
example.programlisting: ... access_prometheus(); # $var(acl_prom) now contains: # # HELP rust_acl_checked rust_acl checked # # TYPE rust_acl_checked counter # rust_acl_checked 12345 # ... ...

section.title: $var(acl_stats)
section.para: JSON string containing the module statistics for the current worker process. Set by access_stats().

section.title: $var(acl_entry_stats)
section.para: JSON array of per-entry match counters for the current worker process. Set by access_entry_stats(). Requires track_counters to be enabled.

section.title: $var(acl_prom)
section.para: Prometheus text exposition format string containing all module statistics. Set by access_prometheus().
Total number of access lookups performed.
Number of lookups that resulted in blocked traffic.
Number of lookups that resulted in allowed traffic.
Number of auto_block() calls.
Number of auto_allow() calls.
Number of entries in the generic blocklist file.
Number of entries in the generic allowlist file.
Number of successful file reloads.
